claim_model: first-come-first-serve; no fixed assignment; claim lowest wave + highest priority with deps done and locks free
validation_default: CGO_ENABLED=1 go test ./...  (== make build vet test)
phase: Backend roadmap COMPLETE (Waves 0-5, T-001..T-120 all landed). Road to 1.0 = Wave 6 launch readiness + Wave 7 product parity & web UI + Wave 8 trust & ecosystem.

completed.title: Adapt + promote protocol to root AGENTS.md
Model proxy: rate caps + audit logging + secret redaction
Production deployment: units + installer + sandbox image
Daemon wiring v2: compose the new subsystems
Egress broker for approved external APIs

tasks.title: SECURITY.md + Private Vulnerability Reporting
acceptance_criteria: SECURITY.md at repo root: supported-versions table, security@ contact, instruction to use PVR as the primary channel
Response-time commitment (initial response <= 14 days), coordinated-disclosure timeline, safe-harbor language, optional PGP key
Private Vulnerability Reporting enabled in repo Settings -> Code security (manual; documented in the issue)
tasks.summary: A security product with no disclosure policy is a trust smell — and both peers (nanoclaw, openclaw) fail GitHub's check here. Ship a SECURITY.md in a recognized location and enable native Private Vulnerability Reporting.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Single most important file for a security product. Bug-report issue template (T-202) must redirect here.

tasks.title: CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md (Contributor Covenant)
acceptance_criteria: CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md at root, Contributor Covenant 2.1, enforcement email filled in (not the placeholder)
Green check on the repo community profile
tasks.summary: Required community-health file; both peers ship one. Adopt Contributor Covenant 2.1 with a real enforcement contact.
tasks.notes_for_worker: GitHub can add this via the web UI in two clicks; commit the result so it is version-controlled.

tasks.title: Issue templates (forms) + PR template
acceptance_criteria: Bug Report + Feature Request as .yml issue forms with valid name/description keys
config.yml: blank_issues_enabled false + contact links (Security policy, Discussions, Discord); bug form redirects security issues to SECURITY.md
pull_request_template.md with a checklist (tests pass, docs updated, contract impact, AGENTS.md scope honored)
tasks.summary: Reduce triage burden and route security reports correctly. Add bug-report + feature-request issue forms, a config.yml that disables blank issues and links Discussions/Discord/Security, and a PR template.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Templates must live in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE with exact key names or GitHub ignores them.

tasks.title: README overhaul: hero command, asciinema cast, security teaser
acceptance_criteria: Hero section: logo, one-line pitch, <=4 meaningful badges (release, license, CI, Discord) — not a wall of shields
One hero install command (the existing install.sh) immediately followed by the first command + API base URL, and a time-to-result phrase
An asciinema cast or terminal GIF embedded; CLI+API-first explicitly framed as a feature; security-model teaser + docs links
tasks.summary: The README is the whole first impression. Lead with logo + one-line pitch, a single hero install command and the exact next command/API URL it yields, a security-model teaser, and links to docs. Since there is no UI, substitute an asciinema cast (install -> first approval -> result) for the missing screenshot and frame CLI+API-first as a feature.
tasks.notes_for_worker: A static screenshot is the one near-universal element a UI-less product lacks; the asciinema recording is the idiomatic fix.

tasks.title: Repo metadata: description, topics, social-preview image
acceptance_criteria: Description set; 8-12 topics (ai-agents, ai-assistant, self-hosted, security, sandbox, golang, claude, personal-assistant, mcp, agent-platform)
1280x640 branded social-preview image committed to docs/assets/ and set in Settings -> Social preview
tasks.summary: Drive discovery and control how the repo looks when shared. Set a crisp description, 8-12 topics, and an Open-Graph social-preview image.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Description/topics are GitHub repo settings (manual); commit the preview asset so it is reproducible.

tasks.title: docker-compose.yml + .env.example + published image
acceptance_criteria: `docker compose up -d` from a clone brings up the control-plane + gateway; .env.example documents every required var
First run mints the admin/API token with a 'claim it / no recovery' warning
A workflow publishes the control-plane image to GHCR on release; README documents the pull
tasks.summary: The most standardized onboarding pattern in the category is `cp .env.example .env && docker compose up -d`. Ship it, and publish the control-plane image to GHCR. The compose topology must reflect the real isolation posture (sandbox network=none), not flatten it.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Coordinate on .github/workflows with T-208/T-253/T-254 via lock:ci/lock:release. Do not weaken network=none in compose.

tasks.title: Guided onboarding wizard (ironctl init / onboard)
acceptance_criteria: `ironctl onboard` runs an idempotent, resumable wizard (detect runtime -> token -> model credential -> sandbox image -> pair one channel -> verify)
Flags --yes (non-interactive) and --dry-run; refuses to overwrite existing config without --force
On success prints the first message to send and the gateway/API URL
tasks.summary: nanoclaw.sh and `openclaw onboard` get a user to a working assistant in minutes; IronClaw leaves env wiring, service install and channel auth as manual steps. Add an interactive `ironctl onboard` that detects the container runtime, mints the API token, registers the Anthropic credential host-side, builds/pulls the sandbox image, pairs a first channel, and verifies end to end.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Pure host-side orchestration over existing APIs; do not touch the frozen contract.

tasks.title: 5-minute Quickstart tutorial
acceptance_criteria: docs/quickstart.md: prerequisites, install, run --dev, submit a change, approve, read audit — each step copy-pasteable
Linked from README and the docs site (T-250)
tasks.summary: A copy-pasteable 'zero to first approved action in 5 minutes' guide using --dev mode (no gVisor), ending at a real approve + audit.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Mirror the local-dev flow already in the inventory; keep it provider-key-light.

tasks.title: Homebrew tap + CHANGELOG + curated release notes
acceptance_criteria: `brew install nivardsec/tap/ironclaw` installs ironctl + control-plane (formula generated from releases)
CHANGELOG.md (Keep a Changelog format) and curated GitHub Release notes templated from commits
tasks.summary: IronClaw's Go binary + existing release pipeline is its distribution edge over the Node/Python peers. A Homebrew tap is the standard instant-install for a CLI; a CHANGELOG is an opensource.guide expectation and a Scorecard signal.
tasks.notes_for_worker: The tap may live in a sibling repo (nivardsec/homebrew-tap); keep the formula source under packaging/homebrew. Coordinate on release.yml via lock:release (T-253).

tasks.title: Public-repo ruleset adapted to the push-to-main flow
acceptance_criteria: Ruleset on main: require CI + CodeQL green, block force-push, require linear history and signed commits — while still permitting direct pushes by authorized actors
Ruleset exported to .github/rulesets/main.json (documented as the source of truth)
tasks.summary: Public repos are expected to protect the default branch, but a hard PR-review gate would break IronClaw's authorized two-agent push-to-main build. Keep the trust signal via required status checks + signed commits + linear history instead of a review gate.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Do NOT add required PR reviews — that contradicts AGENTS.md direct-push CAS. CAS preflight stays the guard.

tasks.title: Support path: Discussions + Discord + seeded good-first-issues
acceptance_criteria: Discussions enabled (Q&A + Announcements); Discord invite linked from README + issue-template config.yml
5-10 real, well-described starter issues labeled 'good first issue' and pinned
tasks.summary: A chat community is mandatory in this category (both peers run a Discord). Enable GitHub Discussions (Q&A + Announcements), stand up a Discord, and seed 5-10 scoped good-first-issues so the tracker does not look inactive.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Mostly GitHub settings; commit a short DISCUSSIONS.md describing which channel is for what.

tasks.title: [spike] Web console architecture + scaffold
acceptance_criteria: Spike doc: stack choice (SPA framework, embed vs separate, auth reuse of the API token, loopback-only binding), security review of serving a UI from the control-plane
Scaffold: web/ app builds and is served by the control-plane at a loopback port behind the existing bearer-token auth; CI builds it
tasks.summary: Every exemplar ships a web dashboard as its primary interface; IronClaw is CLI+API only — its single biggest structural gap. Decide the stack (recommended: an embedded Go-served SPA on a loopback port, like openclaw's Control UI) and land the scaffold that T-221..T-226 build on.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Bind loopback-only by default (cf. openclaw 127.0.0.1:18789). The UI must never widen the network posture. Sole owner of the web/ scaffold; feature tasks own subtrees under it.

tasks.title: Web UI: Approvals inbox
acceptance_criteria: Pending-changes list with per-change detail (kind, group, requester, payload) rendered readably
Approve/reject actions call /v1/changes/{id}/decision; the list updates live
Backed by a read-model endpoint; no new contract surface
tasks.summary: The killer first UI feature for IronClaw: surface the human-approval gateway in a browser. List pending changes, show a readable diff of each requested mutation, and approve/reject with attribution.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Reuses existing gateway endpoints. Disjoint route subtree + one backend file.

tasks.title: Web UI: Sessions browser
acceptance_criteria: Sessions list + detail view; terminate action wired to the host
Read-model endpoint; disjoint route subtree
tasks.summary: List live sessions, inspect status/group/queue depth, and terminate a session — over the existing registry session endpoints.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Builds on /v1/registry/sessions.

tasks.title: Web UI: Channels & wiring management
acceptance_criteria: Create/list wirings and destinations from the UI over existing registry endpoints
Channel credential setup is guided (token fields with redaction)
tasks.summary: A visual surface to connect agent groups to messaging groups (wirings) and manage destinations — the registry CRUD that is CLI-only today.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Mutations still flow through the gateway where applicable.

tasks.title: Web UI: Logs & audit viewer
acceptance_criteria: Audit log view with filter/search/export over /v1/audit
Live tail of structured logs (read-only)
tasks.summary: A real-time, filterable view over the append-only gateway audit log and structured logs (filter by level/module, search, export) — the observability surface every exemplar ships.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Read-only; never expose secrets (reuse obs redaction).

tasks.title: Web UI: Config editor + web onboarding wizard
acceptance_criteria: First-run wizard mirrors `ironctl onboard` steps in the browser
Config editor with validation; every write that mutates capabilities still routes through the gateway
tasks.summary: Browser counterpart to T-206: a first-run wizard (model credential, sandbox runtime, first channel) plus an in-UI config editor with validation — the 'almost everything is done from the UI' expectation (Home Assistant) without weakening the gateway.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Depends on T-206 semantics; do not bypass the approval choke point.

tasks.title: Web UI: Chat playground
acceptance_criteria: Send/receive messages to a chosen agent group via an internal webchat destination
Shows tool calls + token usage inline
tasks.summary: A local test-conversation surface to message an agent group without wiring a real channel — the fastest path to 'see it work' after install.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Implement as a first-class webchat channel adapter feeding the normal router/delivery path.

tasks.title: [spike] Host-side skills / extension system design
acceptance_criteria: Spike doc: a skill manifest format, where skills live (host-curated vs registry), how a skill maps to gateway-approved capability grants, and the supply-chain risk (cf. Koi Security's 341 malicious ClawHub skills)
Recommendation: build / defer / integrate-existing, with a follow-up task breakdown if 'build'
tasks.human_question: Do we add a host-side, gateway-approved skills/extension mechanism (manifest + capability grant), given the 'sealed runtime / no in-sandbox install' pillar? If yes, what is the trust model for third-party skills?
tasks.summary: Skills are the headline extensibility of both peers (nanoclaw /add-* branch-copy model; openclaw SKILL.md + ClawHub, 13.7k+ skills). IronClaw deliberately forbids in-sandbox self-install. A host-side, gateway-approved capability-bundle mechanism could give parity without breaking the seal — but it is an architecture + threat-model decision, not a unilateral build.
tasks.notes_for_worker: SPIKE LANDED 2026-06-17 at .agents/spikes/skills-system.md. Maintainer decision (needs-human): BUILD host-side, gateway-gated capability bundles (manifest + capability grants via the gateway; NO in-sandbox install; third-party skills untrusted; curated+signed source addressing the ClawHub 341-malicious-skills failure). Follow-up task breakdown T-227a..f in the spike.

tasks.title: Channel adapter: WhatsApp
acceptance_criteria: Adapter implements channels.Adapter (send message + file) via a WhatsApp Business/Cloud API or bridge
Token/secret redaction in errors; unit tests with a fake transport
tasks.summary: WhatsApp is the flagship channel for both peers and the top parity ask. Add an adapter implementing channels.Adapter, following the existing telegram.go/slack.go/discord.go pattern.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Registration in main.go is wired by the daemon-wiring owner — do not edit main.go here. Disjoint file from other adapters.

tasks.title: Channel adapter: Email / Gmail
acceptance_criteria: Adapter sends via SMTP/Gmail API and (optionally) ingests via IMAP/Pub/Sub into the inbound path
Credential redaction; unit tests
tasks.summary: Email/Gmail is a first-class channel on both peers (Resend outbound; Gmail Pub/Sub triggers). Add an SMTP/IMAP (or Gmail API) adapter so agents can send and receive mail.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Inbound ingestion may need a small poller on the host side; keep it disjoint from delivery.

tasks.title: Channel adapter: Matrix
acceptance_criteria: Adapter implements channels.Adapter against the Matrix client-server API
Token redaction; unit tests
tasks.summary: Matrix is a privacy-friendly, self-host-aligned channel both peers support. Add a client-server API adapter.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Good first issue — clone the telegram.go shape.

tasks.title: Channel adapter: Google Chat
acceptance_criteria: Adapter implements channels.Adapter for Google Chat spaces
Credential redaction; unit tests
tasks.summary: Google Chat is a common workplace channel on both peers. Add a webhook/bot adapter.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Disjoint file; registration via the daemon-wiring owner.

tasks.title: Additional channel adapters: Teams / iMessage / Signal (tracking)
acceptance_criteria: Teams adapter (bot framework)
iMessage adapter (macOS host bridge)
Signal adapter (signal-cli/bridge)
tasks.summary: Tracking issue to close the long-tail channel gap vs openclaw's 23+ and nanoclaw's 13: Microsoft Teams, iMessage (macOS), Signal. Each is an independent adapter; split into sub-issues when claimed.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Umbrella — prefer one PR per adapter, disjoint files. iMessage is macOS-only.

tasks.title: Multi-provider model support (per-agent provider)
acceptance_criteria: modelproxy allowlist + provider abstraction supports >=1 additional provider behind config, per agent group
Egress stays gateway-governed; audit covers all providers; threat-model sign-off
tasks.human_question: Do we relax the Anthropic-via-proxy posture to allow per-agent providers (OpenAI/Codex, Ollama/local, OpenRouter)? What is the egress + audit story for each, and does the model proxy allowlist expand?
tasks.summary: Peers allow per-agent provider selection via drop-in modules (/add-codex, /add-ollama-provider, OpenCode). IronClaw is Anthropic-via-host-proxy by threat model. Supporting others means new egress destinations through the model proxy — a deliberate threat-model decision.
tasks.notes_for_worker: LANDED 2026-06-17. Maintainer decision (needs-human): FULL multi-provider. Implemented: modelproxy MultiInjector + OpenAI/OpenRouter injectors (host-held key per provider), provider.New factory + OpenAIProvider (Chat Completions), --provider sandbox flag, control-plane enables providers from env keys (union allowlist + redaction), SandboxSpec.Model{Provider,ID,Host} -> oci args, session.Manager.SelectModel resolver, AgentGroup.Provider/Model. Threat-model section 10 documents the posture + sign-off; section 9 non-goal removed.

tasks.title: First-class persona / identity surface
acceptance_criteria: Per-group persona + agent name stored in the registry, set via a gateway-approved change
The sandbox loop loads the persona into the system context; a tool can read (not self-edit) it
tasks.summary: Peers make the assistant feel personal via SOUL.md/CLAUDE.md persona files and a default name (@Andy). IronClaw has a persona change-kind and per-group workspace but no first-class identity surface. Add a per-group persona/identity (name + system persona) editable via the gateway and visible to the agent.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Persona edits are gateway-approved (no in-sandbox self-edit). Confirm no frozen-contract change; if cross-seam, STOP + RFC.

tasks.title: In-product observability: ironctl status / doctor / usage
acceptance_criteria: `ironctl status` summarizes daemon health, live sessions, pending approvals, last delivery
`ironctl doctor` checks runtime/gVisor/token/model-proxy reachability and prints actionable fixes
Token-usage report from model-proxy audit records
tasks.summary: Peers ship rich self-diagnostics (openclaw /status /trace /usage, `openclaw doctor`; nanoclaw chat-based debug). Add `ironctl status` (daemon/session health), `ironctl doctor` (preflight: runtime, gVisor, token, model reachability), and a token-usage report.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Read-only over existing endpoints/metrics. Disjoint files from onboard (T-206).

tasks.title: Documentation site (Mintlify/Fumadocs)
acceptance_criteria: Docs site builds and deploys (CI + a docs.* domain or GitHub Pages)
IA covers quickstart, concepts, self-hosting, config, CLI ref, API ref (T-251), architecture, security model (T-252)
tasks.summary: 7/7 exemplars and both peers run a dedicated docs site; a README alone reads as pre-1.0. Stand up a docs site (Mintlify or Fumadocs) with the consensus IA: Quickstart -> Concepts -> Guides -> Self-Hosting -> Configuration -> CLI + API Reference -> Architecture -> Security Model.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Mintlify ships /llms.txt for free (useful for an AI project). Pull existing docs/*.md in as the starting content.

tasks.title: Publish & check in the OpenAPI spec
acceptance_criteria: Versioned api/openapi.yaml covering every /v1 route with auth/token scopes
Rendered as the API reference in the docs site
tasks.summary: For a UI-less product the OpenAPI spec IS the contract — it documents the API, future-proofs the dashboard (T-22x), and lets third parties build against you. IronClaw already has formal contract discipline; expose the control-plane API as a versioned, checked-in OpenAPI document.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Document the gateway + registry endpoints from internal/host/api. Keep in sync as the API evolves.

tasks.title: Threat-model document expansion (STRIDE + data-flow)
acceptance_criteria: Covers host<->sandbox, control-plane<->agent, agent<->agent, and egress-broker<->network boundaries with STRIDE
Data-flow diagram + privilege matrix; versioned with the code; linked from README and SECURITY.md
tasks.summary: Security is the battlefield for this category and IronClaw's natural high ground. Expand the threat model into a full document: assets, data-flow diagram, trust boundaries, adversaries, a STRIDE pass per boundary, mitigations, and explicit non-goals — also scoping what counts as a 'vulnerability' so disclosure stays sane.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Do not edit docs/contract.md. Build on the existing threat-model.md.

tasks.title: Signed releases + SBOM + provenance (GoReleaser)
acceptance_criteria: GoReleaser builds binaries, generates SBOMs (syft, SPDX + CycloneDX), signs the checksum file keylessly (cosign), emits GitHub artifact attestations for binaries + image
README documents verification (cosign verify / gh attestation verify)
tasks.summary: A security tool that ships unsigned binaries undermines its own thesis. Neither peer offers SBOM/signing — a place IronClaw can decisively win, and the release pipeline already exists. Sign checksums + image (cosign, keyless), emit build provenance attestations, and attach an SBOM to every release.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Acquire lock:release; coordinate with T-205/T-208/T-256 on the release workflow. Mind CGO determinism.

tasks.title: Supply-chain hygiene (Dependabot / CodeQL / secret scanning / pinned actions)
acceptance_criteria: Dependabot alerts + version updates on; CodeQL running on push+PR for Go
Secret scanning + push protection on; every third-party Action pinned to a full commit SHA; top-level permissions: contents: read with per-job elevation
tasks.summary: A compromised dependency or leaked CI secret turns the repo into the attack vector against every downstream user — reputationally fatal for a security vendor. All are free for public repos and map to Scorecard checks; openclaw already runs CodeQL + Dependabot.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Coordinate on .github/workflows via lock:ci. Some toggles are repo settings (document them).

tasks.title: OpenSSF Scorecard + Best Practices badges
acceptance_criteria: Auto-updating Scorecard badge in README with a strong score
Best Practices passing criteria met (note the <=14-day vuln-response and static-analysis-before-release MUSTs)
tasks.summary: The two most recognized at-a-glance trust signals on a security repo, and Scorecard ratifies much of Wave 8. Few peers display either — a differentiator. Run the Scorecard Action (publish_results) and earn the Best Practices passing badge.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Depends in spirit on T-200/T-253/T-254 being in place to score well.

tasks.title: SLSA L3 provenance + reproducible builds
acceptance_criteria: SLSA Build L3 provenance distributed + verifiable (slsa-github-generator)
Deterministic flags (-trimpath, pinned toolchain, SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH) + a documented 'how to reproduce a release' guide (CGO determinism addressed)
tasks.summary: Reproducibility lets a third party rebuild from source and confirm the published binary — the strongest defense against a compromised build server, and the natural complement to signing. Neither peer claims either.
tasks.notes_for_worker: CGO_ENABLED=1 adds C-toolchain determinism considerations. Coordinate on release workflow via lock:release.

tasks.title: Examples gallery + templates
acceptance_criteria: examples/ with >=3 runnable templates (config + README each): e.g. morning briefing, channel triage, multi-agent group
Linked from README + docs
tasks.summary: Every exemplar ships an extensibility system + a gallery and treats a headline count as marketing. An examples/ dir of runnable templates (a scheduled briefing, a triage bot, a multi-agent setup) costs little and seeds adoption.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Use only in-threat-model capabilities (no browser/self-install).

tasks.title: Public roadmap board + demo media + comparison page
acceptance_criteria: Public roadmap (GitHub Projects board or docs/roadmap.md) listing Waves 6-8 incl. Web UI
Demo asciinema cast or short video in README/docs; a 'vs nanoclaw/openclaw' comparison page
tasks.summary: Roadmaps are the weakest area across the field, so a simple public board exceeds it — and is exactly where 'Web UI' should be parked so the gap reads as planned, not missing. Add a public roadmap, demo media (asciinema/video), and a comparison page leaning on IronClaw's signing/SBOM/threat-model edge.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Comparison page should be honest: IronClaw wins on isolation/signing/threat-model; peers win on UI/skills/channel breadth (closing via Wave 7).

tasks.title: Third-party security audit engagement
acceptance_criteria: Engage a recognized firm (consider OSTIF funding) scoped to sandbox isolation, egress broker, RBAC/policy, a2a
Publish the full report in docs/audits/; track remediation publicly; pair with a VDP
tasks.human_question: Approve engaging a third-party security firm (scope, budget, possibly via OSTIF)? Which boundaries are in scope first?
tasks.summary: External validation is the strongest trust signal a security vendor can offer and is often a procurement prerequisite. Neither peer has one — a strong differentiator. Do it after the hygiene items so the audit targets the product, not low-hanging gaps.
tasks.notes_for_worker: Human/budget decision. Sequence after T-200/T-252/T-253/T-254.

tasks.title: End-user credential vault (OneCLI-style request-time injection)
acceptance_criteria: Spike: build-vs-integrate decision; if build, request-time injection over the egress broker with per-agent policy + audit, no raw keys in the sandbox
Threat-model sign-off; agents never see plaintext secrets
tasks.human_question: Do we add an end-user credential vault so agents can call arbitrary approved APIs without holding raw keys (nanoclaw's OneCLI pattern), building on the egress broker? Or integrate onecli rather than build?
tasks.summary: nanoclaw's headline secret story is OneCLI's Agent Vault: agents never hold raw keys; the gateway injects credentials at request time with per-agent policies and rate limits. IronClaw injects only the model credential and now has a gateway-approved egress broker — extending that into a general vault is the parity gap, and a threat-model decision (build vs integrate onecli).
tasks.notes_for_worker: SPIKE LANDED 2026-06-17 at .agents/spikes/credential-vault.md. Maintainer decision (needs-human): INTEGRATE OneCLI as a host-side vault BEHIND the egress broker (broker stays secrets-free per B4-E); do NOT build a bespoke secret-injection path into the broker. Building our own vault stays a non-goal. Follow-up task breakdown T-260a..f in the spike.
